title: Eco-Friendly Living
subtitle: Waste sorting, plastic reduction, safe household chemicals. How to reduce your ecological footprint without complicating life
description: This checklist is a practical guide to greening your daily life without radicalism or guilt. You'll learn which household habits have the greatest impact on reducing your ecological footprint, how to organize waste sorting without cluttering your apartment, what to replace toxic household chemicals with, and how to switch to conscious consumption without sacrificing comfort. Eco-friendly living is not about deprivation, but about smart choices.

items.title: The Reality of Recycling: What Actually Makes Sense to Sort
items.source: EPA — Waste Management Hierarchy & Recycling Reality Report
items.text: The waste hierarchy (Reduce → Reuse → Recycle) sets priorities: recycling is the third, not the first, step. The main goal is reducing consumption. However, of what ends up in the trash, sorting four fractions yields the greatest environmental impact: paper/cardboard (without receipts — they're not recyclable due to thermal paper), PET bottles (marked 1 in the triangle), glass (jars and bottles, except window glass and dishes), and aluminum cans. These materials have a stable secondary raw material market and are actually recycled. The rest — Tetra Pak, soft plastic, composites — most often ends up in a landfill, despite separate collection efforts.
items.tip: Start with two bins: one for mixed waste, the second for the 'golden four' (paper, PET, glass, aluminum). This covers 80% of the impact without complicating your life.

items.title: Plastic Labeling: What the Numbers in the Triangle Mean
items.text: The number inside the triangle on a plastic item is the polymer type code, not a guarantee of recyclability. 1 (PET/PETE) — water and beverage bottles, well recycled. 2 (HDPE) — household chemical bottles, canisters, excellently recycled. 3 (PVC) — window frames, linoleum, toys, NOT recycled and toxic when burned. 4 (LDPE) — bags, film, limited recyclability (special collection points needed). 5 (PP) — yogurt cups, containers, recyclable but not everywhere. 6 (PS) — Styrofoam, disposable tableware, NOT recycled. 7 (Other) — mixed plastics, NOT recycled. When buying a product, check the label and prefer 1, 2, 5.
items.tip: Remember a simple rule: if the plastic is clear and crunchy (water bottle) — recycle it. If it's matte, soft, or foam — general waste.

items.title: Household Chemicals: Toxicology and Safe Alternatives
items.text: Most household chemicals contain substances not tested for long-term health effects. Main toxicants: chlorine (in bleaches — forms organochlorines when contacting organic matter), phosphates (in laundry detergents — cause water eutrophication), synthetic fragrances (phthalates — endocrine disruptors), antibacterial agents (triclosan — contributes to antibiotic resistance). Studies show: indoor air after cleaning with conventional products can be 2-5 times more toxic than outdoor air. Yet for 90% of household tasks, three basic components suffice: baking soda (abrasive and deodorant), citric acid (descaling and rust removal), castile soap (surfactant based on fatty acids).
items.tip: All-purpose cleaner: mix 2 tbsp baking soda, 1 tbsp liquid castile soap, 5 drops tea tree essential oil, and water to a paste consistency. Cleans bathtubs, sinks, and stoves just as well as commercial products.

items.title: The Plastic Bag: Why 5 Minutes of Use Costs 500 Years of Decomposition
items.source: UNEP — Single-Use Plastics: A Roadmap for Sustainability
items.text: The average usage time of a plastic bag is 12 minutes. Its decomposition time in nature is 100 to 500 years (into microplastics; it never fully disappears). About 1 million plastic bags are used worldwide every minute. 8 million tons of plastic enter the ocean annually. The habit of taking a bag at the store seems trivial, but it creates the main volume of plastic pollution. An alternative is a reusable cotton or synthetic shopper. Important nuance: for a cotton shopper to be more eco-friendly than a plastic bag, it must be used at least 150 times (due to cotton's water-intensive production). A synthetic shopper pays off faster.
items.tip: Keep a foldable shopper in your purse or backpack permanently. Post a reminder on your door: 'Keys, phone, wallet, shopper.'

items.title: Microplastics in Cosmetics and Hygiene Products
items.source: Beat the Microbead — Plastic Soup Foundation
items.text: Microplastics — plastic particles less than 5 mm — are intentionally added to scrubs, shower gels, toothpastes, and even lipsticks as abrasives or texturizing agents. These particles pass through water treatment systems and enter rivers and oceans, where they are ingested by plankton and travel up the food chain to humans. Microplastic particles have already been found in the average human body. How to avoid: look for ingredients like polyethylene, polypropylene, acrylates copolymer, nylon-12 on labels. Natural alternatives: scrubs with ground apricot pits, coffee, salt, sugar.
items.tip: Install the Beat the Microbead app — it scans a cosmetic product's barcode and shows if it contains microplastics.

items.title: Water Conservation: Non-Obvious Sources of Consumption
items.source: Water Footprint Network — Household Water Use Data
items.text: It's commonly believed that the main water use is shower and toilet. But there's hidden, virtual water consumption, many times greater than domestic use. Producing 1 kg of beef requires 15,000 liters of water, 1 kg of rice — 2,500 liters, 1 t-shirt — 2,700 liters. Reducing meat consumption by one day a week saves more water than a month of showering instead of baths. This doesn't mean going vegan, but understanding the 'water footprint' of products changes priorities. For household use, the most effective measures: aerators on faucets (reduce consumption by 30-50%), dual-flush toilets, running washing machines and dishwashers only when full.
items.tip: Install aerators on all faucets — it takes 5 minutes and costs little, but water and money savings are noticeable.

items.title: Food Waste: The Main Problem in Home Ecology
items.text: Paradox: about a third of all food produced worldwide is thrown away. At the household level, this is 20-30% of purchased food. Food waste in landfills decomposes without oxygen, releasing methane — a greenhouse gas 25 times more powerful than CO2. Composting organics reduces this effect by 90%. Even in a city apartment, composting is possible using a vermicomposter (with worms) or a disposer (garbage disposal in the sink). But the starting point is meal planning and shopping lists: this reduces spontaneous purchases and subsequent food spoilage.
items.tip: Create a 'leftover meal box': put vegetables and greens that are about to spoil there, and once a week make soup, stew, or smoothies from them.

items.title: Disposable Cups and the 'Coffee to Go' Culture
items.source: The Guardian — Disposable Coffee Cup Investigation
items.text: A paper coffee cup seems eco-friendly, but it's an illusion. To make it waterproof, it's coated with a thin layer of polyethylene, which cannot be separated during recycling. As a result, 99.5% of 'paper' cups end up in landfill, where the paper part decomposes, releasing methane, and the plastic remains for hundreds of years. Global volume is about 500 billion cups per year. The lid and straw worsen the problem. Alternative: a thermos or reusable cup. Many coffee shops offer a discount for bringing your own container.
items.tip: Buy a nice thermos or reusable cup that you like. Carry it with you. In a year, you'll save 200-300 cups from landfill and save money on discounts.

items.title: Conscious Wardrobe: Fast Fashion and Its Ecological Footprint
items.source: Ellen MacArthur Foundation — A New Textiles Economy
items.text: The fashion industry is the second-largest water polluter in the world after agriculture. Producing one cotton t-shirt requires 2,700 liters of water (a person's drinking water for 2.5 years). Synthetic clothing sheds microplastic fibers into the water with every wash. The average lifespan of a garment in developed countries has shrunk from 3-5 years to 5-10 wears. Alternatives: 1) Buy less, but higher quality. 2) Thrift stores and vintage. 3) Repair and customize old clothes. 4) Guppyfriend washing bag to catch microplastics from synthetics.
items.tip: Before buying a new item, ask three questions: 'Do I already have something similar? Will I wear it in 2 years? Can I find this at a thrift store?'

items.title: The Principle of 'Sufficiency' Instead of Perfectionism
items.text: Eco-friendly living often turns into a source of anxiety and guilt: 'I'm doing everything wrong, my plastic still won't be recycled.' This leads to burnout and abandonment of any attempts. The concept of 'sufficiency' offers a different approach: don't aim for zero waste idealism, aim for a meaningful reduction of your footprint. If 1% of the population is perfectly zero waste, the effect will be minuscule. If 50% of the population reduces plastic consumption by 20%, the effect will be huge. Small but sustainable habits of millions change markets and infrastructure.
items.tip: Choose 2-3 eco-habits from this checklist that are easy for you to implement. Do them consistently for a month. Then add new ones. Don't blame yourself for imperfection.

quiz.q: Which four waste fractions have the greatest potential for actual recycling?
a: Tetra Pak, soft plastic, foam, receipts

quiz.q: What does the number 6 (PS) in the triangle on a plastic product mean?
Which three basic components can replace 90% of household chemicals?
a: Baking soda, citric acid, castile soap

quiz.q: Why is a 'paper' coffee cup actually not recyclable?
a: It's coated with a polyethylene layer that cannot be separated
It's too small for sorting machines

quiz.q: What produces more greenhouse effect: food waste in a landfill or an equivalent amount of carbon dioxide?
a: Methane from rotting organics is 25 times more powerful than CO2
